🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Raby Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* County Durham
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 14th century
*Coords:* 54.59083333, -1.80194444
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raby_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Raby%20Castle/@54.62,-1.8419,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNW39uZBXYvhsWYsWQ9OxBpRslavAdWZZR2w0xGoELDGxqktQ_PZSZTi5GNVReOBhzFT2txDwrsKhq_pGdSlwxvDfQoPKnXNnchYGVYEjaAHrtUMx3orepLQI9kmaJ9DFJccEUNk-NBNuY7PZ4=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* https://www.raby.co.uk/raby-castle/ add source 

*Description:*
Raby Castle is a medieval castle located near Staindrop in County Durham, England, among 200 acres (810,000 m2) of deer park. It was built by John Neville, 3rd Baron Neville de Raby, between approximately 1367 and 1390. Cecily Neville, the mother of the Kings Edward IV and Richard III, was born here. After Charles Neville, 6th Earl of Westmorland, led the failed Rising of the North in favour of Mary, Queen of Scots in 1569 Raby Castle was taken into royal custody.

_Listing 1 of 10_

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Chester Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Cheshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 11th century
*Coords:* 53.1853, -2.8923
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chester_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Chester%20Castle/@53.1863,-2.8904,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/places/ANXAkqGwqKs7L78C3hV6r4o_u2wVW8q1ZwZYqdqIlnH-UimqUTayzwD7gI1Luu4DrhDET0qhUtoAfwWIuaeKm2Ua6pwBgKaXSa6QHiM=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Chester Castle is in the city of Chester, Cheshire, England. It is sited at the southwest extremity of the area bounded by the city walls. The castle stands on an eminence overlooking the River Dee. In the castle complex are the remaining parts of the medieval castle together with the neoclassical buildings designed by Thomas Harrison which were built between 1788 and 1813. Parts of the neoclassical buildings are used today by the Crown Court and as a military museum. The museum and the medieval remains are a tourist attraction.

_Listing 2 of 10_

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Lindisfarne Priory
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Northumberland
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 55.68, -1.8025
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lindisfarne
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Lindisfarne%20Priory/@55.669,-1.8008,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZM57izmptxg0fBOodZBmH0R5yZErkgkHHWu52KAV5LtwLyGIEBDdU86T6YW2pHQz_3SeciK9Jx4gzXhkDW4kkvukZ3UFaSIcJUemW24YIOWD-1ASoSQvpVwxtTKzoaPylqQNQEDnJw9OD6ECw=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/Uq6o9nhbRWztg7Mb8

*Description:*
Lindisfarne, also known as Holy Island, is a tidal island off the northeast coast of England, which constitutes the civil parish of Holy Island in Northumberland. Holy Island has a recorded history from the 6th century AD; it was an important centre of Celtic Christianity under Saints Aidan, Cuthbert, Eadfrith, and Eadberht of Lindisfarne. The island was originally home to a monastery, which was destroyed during the Viking invasions but re-established as a priory following the Norman Conquest of England. Other notable sites built on the island are St Mary the Virgin parish church, Lindisfarne Castle, several lighthouses and other navigational markers, and a complex network of lime kilns. The island is part of the Northumberland Coast National Landscape and a hotspot for historical tourism and bird watching.

_Listing 3 of 10_

The island was originally home to a monastery, which was destroyed during the Viking invasions but re-established as a priory following the Norman Conquest of England. Other notable sites built on the island are St Mary the Virgin parish church, Lindisfarne Castle, several lighthouses and other navigational markers, and a complex network of lime kilns. The island is part of the Northumberland Coast National Landscape and a hotspot for historical tourism and bird watching.

*Name:* Auckland Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* County Durham
*Type:* palace
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 54.6664, -1.6702
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auckland_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Auckland%20Castle/@54.6672,-1.6758,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Auckland_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1896444.jpg/330px-Auckland_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1896444.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* unknown
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* Change name to Auckland Palace
Update google places link to https://maps.app.goo.gl/rV3dndqCd6MzxePE6

*Description:*
Auckland Castle, also known as Auckland Palace, is a former bishop's palace located in the town of Bishop Auckland in County Durham, England. The castle was a residence of the bishops of Durham from approximately 1183 and was their primary residence between 1832 and 2012, when the castle and its contents were sold to the Auckland Castle Trust. It is now a tourist attraction, but still houses the bishop's offices.

_Listing 4 of 10_
